summary: McKenzie shares how CrazyEgg's visual heatmap tool revealed a conversion problem on his AdWords landing page that no amount of quantitative analytics could have surfaced. Users were clicking on non-active sidebar text instead of the intended call-to-action button. The fix was trivial — making the bullet points into hyperlinks — but the insight required visual analysis. He emphasizes that CrazyEgg's value lies in making problems visually obvious rather than requiring deep data drilling.
key_points: Visual heatmap tools reveal UX problems that quantitative analytics tools like Google Analytics cannot surface
Users often click on elements that look clickable but aren't, wasting paid traffic
The fix for conversion problems is often trivially simple once you can actually see the problem
Every change should be A/B tested, even 'obvious' fixes — he adds a second line of code for the test
money_quotes: CrazyEgg is really one of my favorite secrets of being a metrics junkie, simply because it makes problems look so freaking obvious, when they could be buried if you relied on quantified analysis.
I paid good money to get folks to see it, and I want them clicking the photo or the big purple button, not clicking the non-active text in the sidebar!
Sure, now that I know the problem is actually happening, fixing it is a matter of adding one line in a Rails template.
One line to make the 'obvious' fix… and another line for the A/B test.
key_insight: Visual analytics tools like heatmaps reveal conversion-killing UX problems that no amount of quantitative data drilling can surface, and the fixes are often trivially simple once you can see the problem.
